turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: submitted We'll hear argument next in Number oh oh twelve forty-nine, Caren Cronk Thomas and Windy City Hemp Development Board versus the Chicago Park District. Mr. Wilson. [Inaudible]

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court, in the unanimous opinion in Freedman versus Maryland this court stated that when the government imposes a permit requirement on the exercise of free speech, that permit scheme must include certain procedural safeguards which are there, quite obviously, to prevent the unwarranted and perhaps unlawful delay or suppression of speech that might occur without them. [Inaudible]

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, Freedman was q- a quite different case from the present one. D-, You agree with that, don't you? I mean, there you're talking about some form of censorship. Here you're prob- talking about a permit to use a park that one-, a lot of other people want to use.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: While I agree that there's a difference between the two cases, this case begs for more protection than the speech involved in Freedman. Because after all, the Freedman case was specifically specifically arose from a censorship scheme which was aimed at sexually explicit speech. This is a case involving core political speech. And although...

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: the... Well...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: is this...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: content neutral...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in your...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: in your view, the regulation?

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: I I believe that the regulation in this case is content...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: neutral.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: It is rife with the opportunity to make viewpoint based decisions, but not on its face.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well I I I I h- h- h- have to say that I, in thinking about the case, I suppose analytically this is a prior restraint.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Justice Kennedy, with respect, I could not disagree with you more, and I think we can look at a few cases to show that this is surely a prior restraint case, and the one that comes to mind most readily is where that exact same question was presented to this Court when, in FW PBS versus The City of Dallas, the Fifth Circuit had held that the Dallas licensing scheme, which was content-neutral, period, in all regards, which a content-neutral licensing scheme licensing sexually oriented business in Dallas, was a time, place, and manner restriction, and this Court rejected that approach and said, first we find that it is an unlawful prior restraint, and therefore...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: it is...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: I think your premise may be one that w- we're going to have trouble adopting.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, there's no indication in this record or in any...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: situation that I'm aware of...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: answer whether that is possible, under First Amendment time, place, and manner doctrine.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Is that not possible?

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Certainly, Justice O'Connor, that's possible.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But aren't you...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: the standards there was a list of thirteen, and they seem to be reasonable, fairly cle- clear standards, and you are coming to us with a facial challenge, and are we to project that those standards will not work properly? You're not coming to us with any concrete case.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, the record in this instance is a solid, concrete case.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And then he was granted it, as I understand it.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Mr. McDonald never received a permit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: for permit...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: conduct his expression.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: He was allowed...

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: to conduct a spontaneous rally on a very limited basis.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: no structures.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: in the list that we have are functions that were being held at the park.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: One of the events in the lodging material?

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Yes, that is true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to go some place else.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: In any...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: point remains that I I I just think you overstate the case when you tell us this is a Freedman case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Now...

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: I think clearly it's a prior restraint.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I mean, there are two...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: disagree with Justice Kennedy's suggestion.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, from Neer versus Minnesota forward, the Court has said that when speech is prohibited in advance, that is a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: prior restraint.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Neer was the shutting down of an, a padlocking of...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: a newspaper of a private...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: y- the the the shoe is on the other foot here.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: My problem with that, Mr. Chief Justice, is not that you have to get in line with other people and and share the use of the park, which was Justice O'Connor's concern when she posed the time, place, and manner re- question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: line will be silent...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: language of, may grant, is something that allows too much discretion? Is that your complaint?

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: It is one of our...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: complaints, and s- even that...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: read may as shall grant, if these conditions are met?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, it says, it says may deny is what it says.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: but maybe it mean means that the permit must be granted if the categories are met.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: But that's not what it says, Justice O'Connor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: creates the potential...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You agree it must be granted if the conditions are met.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You you you agree that if the conditions are met, your, if your client meets all the conditions, he gets in.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: I agree with that, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: What I...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: client doesn't meet some of the conditions, he may not be allowed in, whereas somebody that the park district likes more will be allowed in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's what your complaint,

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: That same thing was true, wasn't it, in Poulos? I mean, the language in Poulos was even more protean than the language in in in in the may phrase.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: I think Poulos w- would have been decided differently by this Court...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: had the...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: got to overrule it to...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to hold...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: your way.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Had had the had the had your had your court, the supreme court of New Hampshire, not construed that statute in such a limiting way to say that if you apply for a permit under Poulos and under Cox you get the permit, there was no discretion to deny, and in the opinion that this Court wrote in Poulos, it pointed that out, that that cured the problem in that case, and I think realistically this Court has recognized...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: wasn't the discretion left the same discretion, in effect, to evaluate facts, and to act based upon that evaluation which the Chicago scheme allowed Chicago?

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: My understanding of that case is that after the construction of the Hamp- the New Hampshire Supreme court placed on it, that became part and parcel of the statute, and even though the statute retained the discretion, it had authoritively[:authoritatively] been limited by the New Hampshire Supreme Court, so the discretion was gone by judicial act and not by legislative change.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The discretion was gone to deny somebody who met the conditions?

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But there, was there no discretion to grant someone who did not meet the conditions? Did the s- New Hampshire supreme court speak to that?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, the interesting...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: the latter that you're complaining about, not...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the former.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: By the way, Justice Scalia, there's another aspect of your inquiry, a- and we both agree, obviously, that if you meet the qualifications, you're entitled to the permit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But that didn't...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: but that didn't happen, and you you say you give us this one instance.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: First, in direct answer to Justice O'Connor's inquiry, a scheme that says you may not obtain a permit for an event at a day and time for which another permit has already been issued, no question.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: W- well but of course that s- that assumes that you're going to have a permit scheme.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: I'm sorry, Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: That that that assumes that you can have a permit scheme.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Surely you can have a permit scheme.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: can have a...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: the what what what the requisites are for the permit scheme.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: i- i- The question may well be, when can you lawfully deny a permit for free speech under this under a permit scheme in what is the traditional public forum, a public park, and what is the most precious of speech, core political speech.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, you're saying the most precious speech.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: No, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: or at another location.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: before the board.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It would seem...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: positing a situation in which somebody has already been granted a permit for the time.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: A first come, first serve rule would certainly help the situation and, of course, these permit applications are dated, time-dated and time-stamped when they are submitted, but there is no requirement that it be first come, first serve.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Well, obviously it's a very difficult question on facts which might never, ever occur, and I'm not claiming that when the park district is making a decision on whether to issue a permit, for space A on day A, it has to look at these permits and say, oops, political speech, first in line,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and that's the diff-

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: If it doesn't say that, doesn't it retain exactly the discretion that you're complaining about?

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: hypothetical  assumes...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: we've got the collision at the door.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Well, I would certainly think that this case need not turn on on that somewhat...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: unlikely occurrence.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: I'd like to know what the principle is that you want us to apply, and I think the principle that you want to us to apply means that when they, that when they bump each other's foreheads at the door, political speech has got to win, or there's an unconstitutional discretion left, amounting to the possibility of a prior restraint.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: I believe that's wrong because well, first of all, a- again, we're not going to find that situation, but let's assume we did, and they bump their heads on the way in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes, but you just keep...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: changing the hypothetical.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: When first come, first serve does not work?

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Yeah, the hypo.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: It would seem that as long as the event is suitable for that particular park, first come, first serve should always work.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: But by hypothesis, Justice Souter's question is that you can't apply it here because they both came at the same time.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Well ju-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You don't have an answer for that, right?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Y- You don't have an answer for that situation. Okay. Yeah,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: yeah, but you think first come, first serve is a is a thoroughly reasonable rule.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: I do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But you're going to tell us that, your brief tell us that...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: When when when when Pope Paul visited John Paul visited Chicago there there was a rally, or a gathering in the Chicago parks.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Justice Scalia...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So that it it finds, gee, you know, the Pope is only going to be here one day, and y- you know, ma- may- maybe maybe we can have this this...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Hemp concert...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Hemp concert later, yeah.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: One one would assume that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: the holder...

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: the holder of the permit would be reasonable in accommodating...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: such as an extraordinary event as this, and if...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: No, no, no this is an unreasonable...

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: I've met them.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, it's it's not Pope John Paul, it's the Beatles, and the Beatles are only going to be there for one day.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: No, sir, and that's why I believe that a first come, first serve rule is going to be reasonable in almost all...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: situations.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: your your brief, you say that even under the first come, first serve rule the Government has the obligation to go to court to validate the permit, under Freedman, and I I just don't get that out of our c-, that's a sure, that's what Freedman said, but Freedman was a very special case, and a time, place, and manner regulation for a park is not.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Well, of course, Freedman ha-, I mean, that they analysis from Freedman has been used in non censorship cases, but in that case the Government needs to bear that burden.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It's a...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Where? You say, the Government goes to court.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: No question, of course, the Supremacy Clause makes Freedman applicable to that State court, but the likelihood that an individual is going to insist on going forward with judicial review when his permit was denied because another permit had already been issued is slight.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No, but I'm asking you...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: I asked you before, what is the scheme that you said would be constitutional, and one part of it surprised me.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: The problem with that, we have brought a facial challenge in this case because of the difficulty, every single time one is refused a permit, in going to State court or Federal court and litigating whether that particular denial was appropriate, and the medicine here is to get rid of the bad ordinance which allows inappropriate and content-based, or viewpoint-based decisions behind closed doors, even if they are not authorized on the face of the scheme.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Do you know any park district...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: that does it that way?

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Well, some have to now.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The govern-

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: a business, a permit to operate a business, not to hold an event in a public park.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, I would...

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: suggest, Justice Ginsburg, that a permit to hold a core political speech rally in a public park deserves at least as much protection as the permit to operate an adult...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: bookstore.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: for content neutral...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: cases there. I say you're, I think...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: you're wrong under our cases there.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: But this Court has said that in cases of public parks, the power of the Government to restrict free speech activity is as, at its most circumscribed.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: And where did we say that?

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: We said that in Hague versus you said that in Hague...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: versus CIO...

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: But no one has ever suggested, Mr. Chief Justice, that that is not the law today, and indeed, that one quotation from Hague appears in core political speeches through this day, and it is one of the most oft-repeated...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: statements from the cases.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: valid today.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: But isn't isn't your argument for applying it this.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: No, it is not, s- Justice Souter.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That's that's why I the there's no need to retreat from Hague, I wouldn't think.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Justice Breyer, i- in order to do that it would seem that this Court would have to retreat from what it said in Forsyth, in which it stated that when a prior restraint in the form of a permit to conduct a political event in a public forum is involved, a facial challenge is appropriate,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and the court...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: nobody denies you can make the challenge.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: My answer is that this is a particular situation where facial challenges are appropriate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: stand I...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: one question about your rule about priorities, the, and it's the basic rule.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Content of the speech.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: One is a one is a baseball game, another is a concert, another is is a lecture on on dinosaurs, and another is political speech.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: If there are competing applications and one of them is a free speech event and one of them is a softball game, and the decision is made based on what the free speech event is urging, what the message is, that is entirely inappropriate.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: It, if you....

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I agree completely.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: But just supposing all you know about it is, they want to make s- it's a political rally of some kind on the one hand, baseball, music, all those can the content, without any hostility to the particular message, be one of the criteria that can can break ties?

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: If your question assumes that each of those events would be appropriate for that specific...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: location...

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: then no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I'd like to reserve the remainder of my time.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Strauss, we'll hear from you.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: has been...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: the earlier application to be accepted,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: at least not as I read it.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Well, first come, first serve if the application is is valid, yes.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: It is required to be accepted? W- Which one of those says that.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Well, it- i- the a valid applica-, a fully executed prior application for a permit has been received, and a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: permit has been granted...

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: That's a reason for denial,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: right.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: doesn't say the converse, that it must be accepted if it's earlier.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Well, the a the , the only ground for denial there are other ground- possible grounds for denial, but one ground for denial is someone was there first.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, it it works out that way because of the provision that says applications shall be processed...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in the order of receipt.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So will effectively be...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: I don't see I really don't see it, and I'm not sure it's commanded, either, but...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I guess...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Do you think that that's constitutionally required that it be a first come, first serve rule?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Well, it is our system, Justice Stevens.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Can you ask for the park three years in advance?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: My understanding, Justice Scalia, is that we have a practice, although I don't know if it's in, if it's written down anywhere, of not accepting applications for more than some period of time in advance,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I think six months.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: There's some some provision...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: whole scheme is written on the basis that the permit may be denied, and there are a set of criteria, but it doesn't appear to be any affirmative requirement that anything be granted if it meets all the requirements.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Oh, Justice O'Connor, we we we do have to grant it if it meets those requirements.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: May deny.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: may deny is an authorization to the park district to deny in these circumstances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And not otherwise.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And and it doesn't do anything to govern how you grant competing applications, other than the fact that you say there is some kind of built in first come, first serve basis.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: The way the competing applications are handled is on a first come, first serve basis.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: One one of the objections made by the petitioner was that either in this case or, reading the regulations, you don't have to give written reasons.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: respond to that.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Justice Kennedy, we do provide reasons.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was my and you did in this case?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Yes, we did, in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: This is...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: based on on previous damage and material misrepresentations in the earlier...

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: application.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: regulations require that you give the reasons?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Yes, they do.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: It then goes on to say that where feasible, thif- if there is a com- if there is a competing use the park district will propose a way to accommodate the accomodate the use.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Could you explain the the degree of discretion to grant, in the event that the conditions are not satisfied?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Yes, Justice Souter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the park district's -- Late.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: A late application, application that's not submitted.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Would there have been discretion in this case? Regar- Let's assume the only prior violation had been the the the fact that if that earlier gathering people were still hanging around at eleven o'clock, after the park or after eleven when the park closed.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Yes, there would have been, if we had concluded that if if if the applicant said to us something that gave us reason to believe it wouldn't happen again, or if the nature of the event were such that if was scheduled earlier in the day, or something like that, that it wouldn't happen again, or if it happened again it wouldn't be so much of a problem, but we don't assert, and I don't think you can assert, consistent with the ordinance, a kind of free floating discretion to overlook violations for people we like and not for people we don't like.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Does the record tell us how many permits are granted and how many denied each year...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: by the park?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, on the grants, the record, the the most precise number we have in the record is there are thousands of applications and thousands of grants a year.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about denials?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Denials, Justice O'Connor, is a hard number to come up with, and this bears on the Freedman versus Maryland point, because what often happens is that the denial takes the form of saying, well we can't accommodate you at this space at this time, but if you're willing to move your event a week later, or willing to move it to this alternative site, then we can accommodate you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: not involving speech.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: a permit was denied to Mr. McDonald based on some prior event where people stayed after eleven o'clock.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, it was denied for multiple reasons.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: There was also...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: administrative mechanisms in place for someone who wants to challenge the basis for the denial...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yup-

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: administratively?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Yes, there is, Justice O'Connor.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: I take it that's the second distinction from Freedman.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Well, the second distinction Freedman while that that is a distinction, Justice Souter, but the second distinction really is that a a a key premise of Freedman is that the decision in question was one that the courts had superior competence to make, and that the agency was to be distrusted in making.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: participation...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Does the same problem get injected by the exception clause in the ordinance, that in fact they can waive conditions if there would be a First Amendment violation?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Justice Souter, that that clause, t- two two things about that clause are are salient, I think.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: and the second thing...

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Which happened here.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: And the third distinction that you want to rely on?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: The third distinction, Justice Souter, is that in the Freedman context the Court had indicated a strong preference for after the fact regulation, that if the co- if a community is concerned about obscenity, the way to regulate that is by after the fact criminal prohibitions.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Strauss, you've ably distinguished Freedman, but do you think that none of the procedural safeguards that were involved in Freedman are applicable here, from here starting with the basic, the the administrator has to have some time limit to act on these petitions to hold events?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, I would put that part of Freedman in a different category.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I I think there's considerable merit, what you have just said, although at the end of your brief you you make the final argument that this is really not a speech statute anyway.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: which, of course...

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Or a zoning permit, variance of some kind, which, of course, apply to expressive activities, but to a lot of other activities, too.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I suppose the easy way to answer...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: that argument is to say we've never done this with reference to parks.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Well, that's right, Justice Kennedy, but as some of the questions from the Court suggested during my colleague's argument, because these are parks, perhaps the Government has more leeway than it would have in telling people what they can do on their own property.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But you did say that you you feel some impulsion from the First Amendment to have a, have to make a decision within a set time to guard against abuse of discretion.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, I think well, for anyone claiming a violation of a constitutional right, there should be an avenue for prompt judicial review, and I , further, I think that part of Freedman is intended to deal the situation like that present in some of this Court's cases, in FW PBS and in Shuttlesworth in particular, a situation where the applicant can't be quite sure when the permit's been denied, so so so the applicant doesn't quite know when it's okay to go to court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: ways. It can also be challenged...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: you have those provisions in means it's a little different from the ordinary zoning ordinance or business license, because those are all motivated by c- First Amendment concerns, I assume.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: prior restraint? Or are you just ducking the issue?

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: No, Justice Stevens, I don't I don't I wouldn't I wouldn't want to duck the issue.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: So if s- if something like this is to be considered valid, what are the limits to make it reasonable as a regulation? We've already mentioned prompt action should be required, perhaps, by the park.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: If the Court does not accept our argument that this is more like a business license,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: then yes, prompt action or a specified time for action by...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: the administrator.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: And an opportunity for prompt judicial review, that's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Otherwise, just, I'm sorry...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: adequacy of judicial review? I take it one objection was that you don't get any actual hearing in court.

turns.name: DAVID A. STRAUSS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, my reading of the Illinois cases is that that's not true even in State court and, of course, the pet- the applicant has the option of going to the Federal court.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Strauss.

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well do you think we should view it as Mr. Strauss urges, as a zoning ordinance, or a business license, or as some kind of content-neutral time, place, and manner restriction?

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: I I think more the latter.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It would make it a lot fairer, wouldn't it? I mean, if if, you either meet the criteria, or you don't.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The a- the the problem would be exactly the...

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: kind of thing that Mr. Strauss described, is that if there is a minor if if you put in your permit application slightly too late, or there was a stray mark on your application, requiring total strictness on any of these criteria would just mean that people who really should be entitled to have the opportunity to speak...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, they didn't foll- follow the rules.

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: But I think...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Why don't you just say, these are the rules,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: if you come in late, you haven't complied with the rules, period.

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: I think as I think as Mr. Strauss I think it was Mr. Strauss said, the the the the that is whatever the flexibility or discretion that remains in a system like this would be exe- has to be exercised in accordance with those grounds for denial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: you can...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: How do you say it doesn't frustrate the purpose of of the provision which says, you know, the the thing has to be in, you know, two weeks beforehand, if you get it in one week beforehand? How can you possibly say that doesn't frustrate the the purpose of the provision? The purpose is to give you two weeks to consider it.

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: Right, but I I think well, you could, it could well be that the purpose is to make sure that the park district can guarantee it can consider it, but if they if it comes in on a day, one day late but there's nothing else on top of it that they're waiting to consider, if it's in the wintertime when they have very few permit applications, they can get to it anyhow, and there's no reason for them not to permit that use of the park.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: In any event, that's...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: from that, I assume there's no way to write a regulation that is not going to require some some judgment, some discretion.

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: I I think that's right, and I think a crucial feature of this requirement is that, if, of the ordinance here is that the parks are used for multiple uses by multiple people.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would it be...

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: That should be addressed on a on a on an as applied basis, and and I think it could be in the State court proceedings, or in a nineteen eighty-three proceeding.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Along the lines of Justice Scalia's question, would it be proper for a city council or municipality to draft an ordinance just like this one and then, then at the end saying, the commissioner of parks, in his sole discretion, may waive any or all of the foregoing requirements?

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: I think the the only difficul- it would depend on what that meant.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, we've said in in a case like Forsyth that the s- discretion has to has to have s- h- have some definition and some control to it, and if you have at the end an an absolute waiver provision, s- it seems to me that would contradict that.

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: Oh, I I think well, I think that's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: guided...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: How do, where where does it say that that the may, the may grant anyway is limited to those applications that generally meet the purposes of the st-, where does it say that? Do we just take your word for it?

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or or the park district's word for it?

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: I think that that would at least be the most reasonable construction of an ordinance like this, where there's a- a- attempt to detail these thirteen specific criteria quite specifically, and there's no suggestion that there's any other basis on which the park district can act, and it it, I I think that that's just the most reasonable interpretation of this kind of ordinance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to all.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Federal regulations for the use of Federal parks, the use of the Mall, do they differ with respect to the may deny?

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But there is the...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: the may deny, implying that there are cases where, although you could, you don't have to deny?

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And and no criteria for the waiver in the Federal s-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in the Federal scheme either?

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: way those are, have consistently been applied is, those are the grounds that are suppose, to be considered i- in determining whether you can grant a permit, and there aren't other grounds on which a permit would be denied.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And and i- is there a first come, first serve rule?

turns.name: JAMES A. FELDMAN
turns.text: Basically, yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Feldman.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, I'd like to address quickly two points that you raised.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: prompt judicial review...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: does that mean if you pick the Fede- Federal court, that Illinois is going to write a statute that says, and if you choose to come to the Federal court rather than the State court, the Federal court is going to have X number of days to decide it?

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, that would be the choice of the park district of the City of Chicago, because the park district is the is the party that has to go to court to seek...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Oh, if if...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: you're but that's not what Justice O'Connor said in in her case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: take that last part of Freedman.

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: But there's certainly a distinction between those businesses that Justice O'Connor was writing about in FW PBS and a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: core political speech.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: your notion that the scheme is invalid unless the park goes to court, and the court has a tight time line?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: We...

turns.name: RICHARD L. WILSON
turns.text: I would then say, Justice Ginsburg, that this Court has elevated the kind of sexually explicit speech in that case above the core political speech in this case, based on the content of the speech, which would fly in the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: face of...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: follow that at all.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Wilson.

turns.name: THE MARSHAL
turns.text: The honorable court is now adjourned until tomorrow at ten o'clock.
